questions.stem: Which organelle is the main site of aerobic respiration and ATP production in a cell?
questions.explanation: The mitochondrion is the site of aerobic respiration, where glucose products are oxidized and most of the cell's ATP is generated. Ribosomes build proteins, the Golgi apparatus modifies and packages proteins, and lysosomes carry out digestion. Because of its energy role the mitochondrion is often called the powerhouse of the cell.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: What is the basic structural and functional unit of all living organisms?
questions.explanation: Cell theory states that the cell is the basic unit of structure and function in living things, that all organisms are made of one or more cells, and that all cells arise from pre-existing cells. Tissues and organs are higher levels of organization built from cells, and molecules are below the level of the cell.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which feature is present in plant cells but absent in typical animal cells?
questions.explanation: Plant cells have a rigid cell wall made largely of cellulose outside the cell membrane, while animal cells do not. The nucleus, cell membrane, and mitochondria are found in both plant and animal cells, so they cannot be the distinguishing feature here.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which structure controls the activities of the cell and contains most of its genetic material?
questions.explanation: The nucleus houses the cell's chromosomes (DNA) and directs cell activities such as growth, metabolism, and reproduction by controlling gene expression. The vacuole stores materials, the cytoplasm is the fluid filling the cell, and the cell membrane controls what enters and leaves.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which organelle is the site of photosynthesis in plant cells?
questions.explanation: Chloroplasts contain the green pigment chlorophyll and carry out photosynthesis, using light energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose. Mitochondria release energy by respiration, the nucleus stores genetic information, and ribosomes make proteins.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: What is the primary function of ribosomes?
questions.explanation: Ribosomes are the sites of protein synthesis, where amino acids are joined into polypeptide chains according to the sequence specified by messenger RNA. Lipid storage, DNA replication in the nucleus, and respiration in the mitochondria are handled by other structures.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes a prokaryotic cell?
options: It is found only in animals
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells, such as bacteria, lack a true membrane-bound nucleus and lack membrane-bound organelles; their genetic material lies free in the cytoplasm. Chloroplasts are found in plant and algal eukaryotes, not as a defining feature of prokaryotes, and prokaryotes are not restricted to animals.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: The cell membrane is best described by which model of its structure?
questions.explanation: The fluid mosaic model describes the cell membrane as a fluid phospholipid bilayer with proteins embedded in or attached to it, like a mosaic. The lock and key and induced fit models describe enzyme action, and the double helix describes DNA structure.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which type of molecule forms the basic bilayer of the cell membrane?
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is built mainly from a phospholipid bilayer. Each phospholipid has a hydrophilic phosphate head and two hydrophobic fatty acid tails, so the molecules arrange in two layers with the tails facing inward. Nucleic acids, simple sugars, and free amino acids do not form this bilayer.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which organelle is responsible for modifying, sorting, and packaging proteins for secretion or delivery within the cell?
questions.explanation: The Golgi apparatus receives proteins from the endoplasmic reticulum, modifies them, and packages them into vesicles for transport to their destinations. The nucleolus makes ribosome components, the smooth endoplasmic reticulum makes lipids, and centrioles help organize cell division.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Lysosomes contain digestive enzymes. What is their main role in the cell?
options: Breaking down waste materials and worn-out cell parts
questions.explanation: Lysosomes contain hydrolytic (digestive) enzymes that break down waste, foreign particles, and worn-out organelles, recycling the materials. ATP production occurs in mitochondria, protein synthesis at ribosomes, and genetic information is stored in the nucleus.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which structure surrounds and is found inside the nucleolus, and what does the nucleolus produce?
questions.explanation: The nucleolus is a dense region inside the nucleus where ribosomal RNA is made and assembled with proteins into ribosome subunits, which then move out to the cytoplasm. It does not make ATP, digest bacteria, or store starch.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: What is the role of the rough endoplasmic reticulum?
options: It generates ATP for the cell
It transports proteins made by attached ribosomes
questions.explanation: The rough endoplasmic reticulum is studded with ribosomes and helps process and transport the proteins those ribosomes make. Photosynthesis occurs in chloroplasts, DNA is stored in the nucleus, and ATP is produced in mitochondria.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Movement of water across a selectively permeable membrane from a region of higher water concentration to a region of lower water concentration is called what?
questions.explanation: Osmosis is the diffusion of water across a selectively permeable membrane from a region of higher water concentration (lower solute) to a region of lower water concentration (higher solute). Active transport moves substances against a gradient using energy, while photosynthesis and respiration are metabolic processes.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which process moves substances across a membrane against their concentration gradient and therefore requires energy?
questions.explanation: Active transport moves substances from a region of lower concentration to a region of higher concentration, against the gradient, and so requires energy from ATP. Simple diffusion, osmosis, and facilitated diffusion are passive and move substances down their gradients without energy input.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: An animal cell is placed in pure water (a hypotonic solution). What is most likely to happen?
options: Water leaves the cell and it shrinks
Water enters the cell and it may swell or burst
Nothing changes because the membrane blocks water
The cell wall keeps the cell rigid
questions.explanation: In a hypotonic solution the surrounding water concentration is higher than inside the cell, so water moves in by osmosis. An animal cell has no cell wall to resist the pressure, so it swells and can burst (lyse). It does not shrink, and animal cells have no cell wall.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which phase of mitosis is characterized by chromosomes lining up along the middle (equator) of the cell?
questions.explanation: During metaphase the chromosomes align along the metaphase plate at the cell's equator, ready to be separated. In prophase chromosomes condense, in telophase nuclei reform, and interphase is the growth stage between divisions.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Mitosis produces daughter cells that are genetically identical to the parent cell. How many daughter cells result from one mitotic division?
questions.explanation: A single mitotic division produces two daughter cells, each with the same chromosome number and genetic content as the parent cell. Meiosis, by contrast, produces four genetically varied cells with half the chromosome number.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Meiosis is important in sexually reproducing organisms because it produces cells with which chromosome number?
options: Twice the chromosome number of the parent cell
Half the chromosome number of the parent cell
The same chromosome number as the parent cell
questions.explanation: Meiosis is a reduction division that halves the chromosome number, producing haploid gametes from a diploid parent cell. When two gametes fuse at fertilization, the normal chromosome number is restored. Mitosis, not meiosis, keeps the chromosome number the same.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which large, fluid-filled organelle is especially prominent in mature plant cells and helps maintain their shape through internal pressure?
questions.explanation: Mature plant cells usually contain a large central vacuole filled with cell sap. When full it presses against the cell wall, creating turgor pressure that supports the cell and helps keep the plant firm. Lysosomes, centrioles, and ribosomes do not serve this role.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: The control of which substances enter and leave the cell is mainly the function of which structure?
questions.explanation: The cell membrane is selectively permeable, regulating which substances pass into and out of the cell. The nucleolus makes ribosome parts, the Golgi apparatus packages proteins, and the mitochondrion releases energy, so none of these controls membrane transport.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which of the following is a correct statement of part of the cell theory?
options: Only animals are made of cells
Cells can form spontaneously from non-living matter
All cells arise from pre-existing cells
A cell is larger than any tissue
questions.explanation: A central part of cell theory is that all cells come from pre-existing cells by division. Spontaneous generation of cells from non-living matter was disproven, all living organisms (not only animals) are made of cells, and tissues are made of many cells so a cell is smaller than a tissue.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: DNA replication and the copying of the genetic material in a eukaryotic cell occur mainly during which part of the cell cycle?
questions.explanation: DNA is replicated during the S (synthesis) phase of interphase, before the cell enters mitosis. Metaphase and anaphase are stages of mitosis that separate already copied chromosomes, and cytokinesis divides the cytoplasm at the end of division.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text

questions.stem: Which statement correctly compares the size of a typical prokaryotic cell with a typical eukaryotic cell?
options: Both are always exactly the same size
Prokaryotic cells are visible to the naked eye while eukaryotic cells are not
Prokaryotic cells are generally smaller than eukaryotic cells
Prokaryotic cells are generally larger than eukaryotic cells
questions.explanation: Prokaryotic cells such as bacteria are generally much smaller and simpler than eukaryotic cells, which contain a nucleus and many organelles. Most individual cells of either type are microscopic and not visible to the naked eye.
questions.source: in the style of a standard introductory biology text
